directions.name: Deepen single-skew cp.async overlap in the fixed peeled 64x384 hot kernel
directions.hypothesis: The current best result already confirms the right foundation: peeled fixed-shape hot loop plus trimmed export path. Relative to the older accepted base, tensor active is up at 34.53% and barrier stall is down at 6.39%, so the remaining gap is no longer control-heavy. What still stands out is 14.23% short-scoreboard, 30.42% mio_throttle, about 41.77% DRAM throughput, and about 82.75% LSU request pressure on the hot peeled kernel. The best next move is therefore a bounded overlap retune inside the existing single-skew peeled loop: keep the 64x384 tile and current layout, but rework commit/wait timing, prefill placement, or stage usage so more of the peeled loop runs against in-flight copies instead of exposed latency.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Copy-latency exposure and in-loop feed overlap in the peeled 64x384 hot kernel.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:41-58 (current shared-memory budget and stage-count constraints)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:567-605 (peeled hot-loop prefill, next-stage issue, and wait/barrier sequence)
directions.risk: The current kernel is already a new best, so overlap retuning can easily move the pipeline the wrong way and give back the peeled-hot-path win without a large enough upside.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if short-scoreboard and mio_throttle do not fall on top of the current peeled kernel, or if runtime regresses while barrier stall stays low.

directions.name: Peel and specialize the fixed 64x96 tail kernel to match the hot path
directions.hypothesis: The hot 64x384 path is now fixed-shape peeled, but the 64x96 tail still falls back to the generic tensor-core kernel and launch path. That tail is only one CTA column wide, so it is not the dominant cost, but it is now the most obviously un-specialized part of the fixed benchmark pipeline. Its own profile is still generic-path flavored, with about 19.73% barrier stall and 15.36% short-scoreboard. A peeled fixed-K tail kernel with the same fixed-shape assumptions and quad export style as the hot kernel is a coherent next move because it complements the winning direction rather than reopening the failed B-feed or live-set rewrites.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual fixed-shape tail overhead from using the generic 64x96 tensor-core kernel on the last 96 columns.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:409-526 (current generic tensor-core kernel that still serves the 64x96 tail)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:528-666 (fixed peeled hot-kernel template that the tail can mirror in smaller form)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:689-722 (fixed-shape dispatch that currently launches the generic TensorCoreTile96 tail)
directions.risk: The payoff is bounded because the tail covers only 96 of 7776 columns, so even a clean tail specialization may save less time than hot-kernel work.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the specialized tail does not show a measurable end-to-end runtime gain or if the tail-only improvement is below noise.

directions.name: Try a bounded single-skew B-stride retune on the peeled hot kernel
directions.hypothesis: Recent history rules out broad feed-path rewrites: the two-level B repack regressed badly, and warp-specialized staging also lost badly even when it improved one symptom. Still, the new best peeled kernel continues to show high DRAM and LSU pressure with the same single-skew B layout. That leaves one narrowly bounded feed-side follow-up worth keeping in the set: preserve the row-major single-skew semantics and the peeled hot loop, but probe whether a modest stride/skew adjustment can shave residual B-side pressure without reopening the failed producer-layout redesign family.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual B-side staging pressure inside the current single-skew peeled hot kernel, not a full feed-path redesign.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:47-52 (current single-skew B shared stride and tile sizing)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:207-239 (logical-to-shared B mapping and staging path)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:592-600 (peeled hot-kernel B fragment loads from the staged layout)
directions.risk: This sits near a recently failed family, so the chance of regressing the new best base is high unless the change stays tightly bounded.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if DRAM / LSU pressure does not improve quickly or if the bounded stride retune loses the current best runtime.

notes: Round 1/5 diagnosis prepared from the current best custom run 20260419_101256_bf16_gemm_v1_5ab5302 at 36.517889 ms. Recent history is applied explicitly: broad B-feed rewrites and phased live-set rewrites regressed, while the winning move was peeled hot-kernel specialization plus trimmed export. Ranking therefore builds on the current peeled 64x384 kernel and favors complementary overlap, tail specialization, and only a tightly bounded single-skew B follow-up.
